summary: IPC contract-drift audit (Rust<->schema<->C#) + engine/DB/ML performance audit for the 4GB target
logs: IPC+perf audit done: 9 confirmed, 1 uncertain (of 12 raw)

confirmed.title: Large restructurePlan event exceeds the 1 MB C# read-frame cap and is silently discarded
confirmed.description: The engine emits the entire Restructure plan as ONE `restructurePlan` event whose `_0.moves` array embeds every proposed move (commands/restructure.rs:228-264, sent via a single sink.send). Each RestructureMove carries fileID + two absolute Windows paths (source, destination) + category + tier + confidence + reason — realistically ~250-400 bytes of JSON per move. The C# stdout reader caps a single frame at `MaxFrameChars = 1024*1024` (EngineClient.cs:58) and, when `st.Buffer.Length > MaxFrameChars` with no newline yet (line 603), it clears the buffer, sets `Resyncing=true`, drops the whole frame, and emits only a `DebugLog.Warn` (line 605). The engine's sink write path (ipc/sink.rs:81-88 `write_frame`) has NO symmetric outbound cap, so it happily writes the multi-MB line. The cap comment (EngineClient.cs:55-57) says it 'Mirrors the engine's 1 MiB MAX_FRAME_BYTES guard' — but that engine guard (main.rs:330 `MAX_FRAME_BYTES`, bounded_read.rs) only bounds INBOUND commands (app->engine), where payloads are small by design; it does not bound OUTBOUND events. The two caps thus protect opposite directions, and the only unbounded-payload event (restructurePlan) falls through.
confirmed.impact: On a library of more than ~3,000-3,500 proposed moves the restructurePlan frame crosses 1 MB and is discarded. RestructurePlanEvent never reaches EngineClient.Apply (EngineClient.cs:1137-1138), so `LastRestructurePlan` stays null and the Restructure tab (one of the six headline features) renders empty with no user-visible error — only a debug log. The project explicitly targets 'tens of thousands of files', so a real user library hits this. It is a hard functional cliff, not a slowdown.
confirmed.fix: Either (a) raise the C# events cap well above the realistic plan size and/or make the engine chunk/stream the plan in bounded batches (e.g. paged restructurePlan events the app accumulates), or (b) make the engine cap its own outbound restructurePlan size and page it. At minimum, when ReadBoundedFrameAsync drops an oversize frame, surface a user-visible error (an EngineError-style toast) instead of a silent DebugLog.Warn so the empty Restructure tab is explained. Note the cap is on chars in C# vs bytes in Rust, a second minor asymmetry.
confirmed.evidence: EngineClient.cs:58 `private const int MaxFrameChars = 1024 * 1024;`; EngineClient.cs:603-607 oversize discard+resync with only DebugLog.Warn (605); commands/restructure.rs:228-264 builds full moves vec and `sink.send(...RestructurePlan(Wrap::new(plan)))` once; ipc/sink.rs:81-88 write_frame has no size cap; main.rs:330 MAX_FRAME_BYTES applies only to bounded_read_line of inbound commands. RestructureMove fields confirmed in ipc/mod.rs:271-291 (source, destination, category, tier, confidence, reason).

verdict.reasoning:
Independently verified all load-bearing claims against the actual source.

1. C# cap + silent drop: EngineClient.cs:58 `private const int MaxFrameChars = 1024 * 1024;`. EngineClient.cs:603-607 — when Buffer.Length > MaxFrameChars with no newline, it logs only `DebugLog.Warn` (605), Buffer.Clear() (606), Resyncing=true (607). The resync at 591-596 then ALSO drops the tail-of-line frame. So the entire oversize event is discarded with no user-visible error.

2. Single path, no bypass: StdoutLoopAsync routes EVERY stdout frame through ReadBoundedFrameAsync (EngineClient.cs:650). No large-event bypass exists.

3. Engine sends whole plan as ONE event: restructure.rs:228-264 builds the full `moves` vec (one IpcMove per proposed move) and calls `sink.send(...EventPayload::RestructurePlan(Wrap::new(plan)))` exactly once (line 263).

4. No upstream move cap: PLAN_FILES_SQL (restructure.rs:23-34) = `SELECT ... FROM files f WHERE f.failed = 0` with NO LIMIT. Every eligible non-failed file is classified into a move; moves.len() scales with library size.

5. Engine outbound write has NO size cap: sink.rs:81-88 write_frame is just serde_json::to_writer + newline + flush. No length guard, so it writes a multi-MB line happily.

6. MAX_FRAME_BYTES bounds INBOUND only: main.rs:330 const MAX_FRAME_BYTES = 1024*1024 is used solely at main.rs:339 in bounded_read_line for app->engine command reads. The C# comment (EngineClient.cs:55-57) claiming it 'Mirrors the engine's MAX_FRAME_BYTES guard' is misleading — the two caps guard OPPOSITE directions; the unbounded-payload outbound event (restructurePlan) is unprotected on both ends.

7. RestructureMove fields confirmed at ipc/mod.rs:271-291: fileID, source, destination, category, tier, confidence, reason. Two absolute Windows paths per move; in JSON every '\' is escaped to '\\', doubling backslashes. ~250-400 bytes/move is realistic and conservative.

8. Apply consequence: EngineClient.cs:1137-1138 — RestructurePlanEvent sets LastRestructurePlan. Dropped frame => Apply never runs => LastRestructurePlan stays null => Restructure tab renders empty with only a debug log.

9. Schema check: ipc.schema.json has exactly one restructurePlan event (lines 290, 619-663); there is NO paged/streaming plan variant to fall back on.

Threshold math: 1,048,576 chars / ~300 bytes ≈ 3,495 moves (range 2,620–4,195 across 400–250 bytes/move). Matches the finding's '~3,000-3,500'. Root CLAUDE.md explicitly targets 'tens of thousands of files', so a real library of 10k+ image/doc files trivially exceeds the cap. ASCII paths are 1 UTF-16 char/byte, so the char-vs-byte distinction does not save it (and for non-ASCII the char cap is slightly more generous, never less).

This is a hard functional cliff (empty tab + silent failure above a realistic library size), not a perf slowdown, and it is on the only code path that delivers the plan.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct in direction and the diagnosis is accurate. Engine-side paging is the robust answer because the plan payload is inherently unbounded (one move per file, no LIMIT in PLAN_FILES_SQL). Simply raising the C# cap only pushes the cliff out; a 30k-file library at ~300 B/move is ~9 MB and would still need a much larger cap AND a corresponding rethink of holding the whole plan in one StringBuilder frame on the 4 GB target. The minimum mitigation in the finding — surface an EngineError-style toast on oversize-frame drop instead of a silent DebugLog.Warn — is valid and cheap, and should be done regardless so the empty tab is never silent. One nuance to correct in the finding's framing: the char-vs-byte asymmetry is NOT a second failure-direction cliff — ASCII paths map 1 char:1 byte, and multibyte UTF-8 paths make the char cap MORE generous than the byte size, so it never worsens the drop threshold. The finding already labels it 'minor', which is fair.
verdict.corrected_fix:
Preferred: page the plan engine-side. Add a bounded `restructurePlanPage` event (or reuse restructurePlan with an explicit {page, totalPages, isFinal} envelope) carrying e.g. 1,000 moves per frame, keeping each frame well under 1 MiB; the app accumulates pages into LastRestructurePlan and renders the Sankey once isFinal arrives. This lands in shared/ipc-schema/ipc.schema.json first (new event def + variant), then the Rust DTO in ipc/mod.rs and the C# mirror in EventPayload.cs/Dtos.cs, per 'the IPC contract is the contract'. The engine emits pages from restructure.rs by chunking the proposed moves vec before building IpcMove batches.

Minimum / immediate: in ReadBoundedFrameAsync's oversize branch (EngineClient.cs:603-607), in addition to DebugLog.Warn, raise a user-visible EngineError/toast (the same channel EngineError events use) so the empty Restructure tab is explained rather than silently blank. Also fix the misleading comment at EngineClient.cs:55-57 — clarify that MAX_FRAME_BYTES bounds INBOUND commands only and that the C# cap bounds INBOUND events, i.e. they are not symmetric guards of the same stream.

Do NOT rely solely on bumping MaxFrameChars: with 'tens of thousands of files' the plan can reach many MB, and on the 4 GB DirectML target buffering the whole plan as one StringBuilder frame plus the parsed object graph is itself memory pressure — paging avoids both the cliff and the peak.

confirmed.title: Unknown enum string value drops the entire event; unknown object fields are tolerated (forward-compat asymmetry)
confirmed.description: Strict string enums (ScanPhase, JobCategory, LogLevel, DeepAnalyzeStartingPhase — registered as JsonStringEnumConverter at IpcCoder.cs:67-70, with no fallback/unknown handling) THROW JsonException on any value outside the known set. Because EngineClient.StdoutLoopAsync wraps IpcCoder.Decode in try/catch+continue (EngineClient.cs:670-678), an unknown enum value drops the ENTIRE event, not just the offending field. By contrast, unknown OBJECT fields on a payload are silently ignored (additive forward-compat works). I verified this empirically: feeding `{"phaseChanged":{"_0":"warpDrive"}}` through the real IpcCoder.Decode<IpcEvent> throws 'The JSON value could not be converted to FileID.IpcSchema.ScanPhase', while a queueState payload with an extra unknown field decodes fine. The same strictness exists symmetrically in Rust serde and in the command direction (e.g. ApplyTagsCommand.Mode is a plain string in C# but a strict TagMode enum in Rust — an out-of-set mode would make handle_line silently drop the whole tag command).
confirmed.impact: No active drift today: all enum value sets currently match across schema/Rust/C# (verified). But the schema comments anticipate engine evolution (e.g. EngineError.kind growing). The moment a newer engine adds a value to any of these four strict enums, an older app drops every event carrying it (e.g. a new ScanPhase would drop all progress/phaseChanged events for that phase) instead of degrading. Note kind/action-style discriminators that DO grow (EngineError.kind, FileDoneEvent.kind, BulkActionResult.action) are correctly modeled as plain strings; only these four are strict.
confirmed.fix: If forward-compat of enum-carrying events matters, give the strict enums an unknown/default fallback variant (e.g. a [JsonStringEnumMemberName]-style 'unknown' catch, or a custom converter that maps unrecognized strings to a sentinel) rather than throwing. Otherwise document that any new enum value is a breaking change requiring app+engine ship in lockstep.
confirmed.evidence: IpcCoder.cs:67-70 registers JsonStringEnumConverter with a naming policy but no unknown-value tolerance; empirical decode test: input `{"t":"...","payload":{"phaseChanged":{"_0":"warpDrive"}}}` -> THROW JsonException, vs `{"queueState":{"_0":{"running":null,"pending":[],"newFutureField":99}}}` -> OK; EngineClient.cs:674-678 catches and `continue`s.

verdict.reasoning:
Independently verified all load-bearing claims against the actual three sources.

1. IpcCoder.cs:67-70 — confirmed: registers JsonStringEnumConverter<ScanPhase/JobCategory/LogLevel/DeepAnalyzeStartingPhase> with only a CamelCase naming policy. No fallback/unknown handling, no allowIntegerValues override, and BuildOptions sets no UnmappedMemberHandling. .NET 8 JsonStringEnumConverter<T> throws JsonException on any string outside the known member set.

2. EngineClient.StdoutLoopAsync (ViewModels/EngineClient.cs:669-678) — confirmed: IpcCoder.Decode<IpcEvent> is wrapped in try/catch that logs a warn and `continue`s. So an unknown enum value aborts deserialization of the ENTIRE frame, dropping the whole event, not just the field. PhaseChangedEvent (EventPayload.cs:21,79) decodes ScanPhase via ReadWrapped<ScanPhase>, which routes through the strict converter — so the cited `{"phaseChanged":{"_0":"warpDrive"}}` throw is accurate.

3. No active drift today — confirmed by reading all three sources. ScanPhase: schema (ipc.schema.json:798) ["idle","discovering","tagging","postScan","completed","cancelled","failed"] == Rust mod.rs:671-679 (rename_all=camelCase) == C# Common.cs:11-20. LogLevel: schema:477 ["debug","info","warn","error"] == Rust:784 (lowercase) == C# Common.cs:29-35. JobCategory: schema:807 ["scan","faceCluster","deepAnalyze"] == Rust:883 == C# Common.cs:22-27. DeepAnalyzeStartingPhase: schema:514 ["queued","loadingModel","resolvingTargets"] == Rust:805-809 == C# Common.cs:37-42. All four enum value sets match exactly.

4. Forward-compat asymmetry — confirmed. Unknown OBJECT fields are tolerated at runtime: C# System.Text.Json defaults to skipping unmapped members (IpcCoder sets no UnmappedMemberHandling.Disallow), and Rust serde has no deny_unknown_fields anywhere in mod.rs (grep empty). Note: the schema's 130 additionalProperties:false occurrences are schema-validation constraints and do NOT govern runtime decode, so they don't contradict the finding. So unknown fields = tolerated, unknown enum strings = fatal-to-whole-frame. The asymmetry is real.

5. Command-direction symmetry — confirmed. ApplyTagsCommand.Mode is `string Mode = "add"` (CommandPayload.cs:85, plain string, tolerant) while Rust TagMode (mod.rs:304-314, rename_all=lowercase, set {add,replace,remove}) is a strict enum. main::handle_line (main.rs:486-495) drops the ENTIRE command on any serde parse failure (returns early after a silenced warn). So an out-of-set mode silently drops the whole tag command. Schema mode enum (ipc.schema.json:156) is ["add","replace","remove"] — matches.

6. The grow-able discriminators are correctly modeled as plain strings: EngineError.kind (Rust mod.rs:764 String; C# Dtos.cs:153 string; schema comment anticipates growth), FileDoneEvent.Kind (Dtos.cs:119 string), BulkActionResult.Action (Dtos.cs:42 string). Confirmed — only the four named enums are strict.

This is a genuine forward-compat/decode-robustness observation, correctly scoped. It is NOT active drift (all sets match) and NOT a perf cliff (decode of a malformed frame is off the steady-state hot path and rare). It is a real asymmetry in how the contract degrades under future evolution.
verdict.fix_assessment: Direction is correct; one detail is imprecise. A custom tolerant JsonConverter<T> mapping unrecognized strings to a sentinel variant is the right C#-side fix, and documenting that any new enum value otherwise requires lockstep app+engine ship is the right alternative. However, the finding's parenthetical "[JsonStringEnumMemberName]-style 'unknown' catch" is slightly wrong: JsonStringEnumMemberName (.NET 9) renames a KNOWN member's wire string; it is not a catch-all for unknown inputs. The accurate fix is (a) add an `Unknown` sentinel to each of the four C# enums plus a custom JsonConverter<T> that returns it for unrecognized strings, and (b) for true symmetry, give the Rust enums an `#[serde(other)] Unknown` variant so the engine degrades the same way on the command direction (e.g. TagMode). Mitigating context worth noting in any writeup: the app and engine ship as a single Authenticode-signed bundle, so version skew between them is uncommon in practice, which is why low severity is right.
verdict.corrected_fix: If forward-compat of enum-carrying frames matters: add an explicit `Unknown` sentinel member to each strict enum (ScanPhase, JobCategory, LogLevel, DeepAnalyzeStartingPhase) and register a custom JsonConverter<T> per enum that maps any unrecognized string to that sentinel instead of throwing (do NOT rely on [JsonStringEnumMemberName], which only renames known members). For symmetric robustness on the command direction, add `#[serde(other)] Unknown` to the Rust TagMode enum (and any other strict command-side enums) so handle_line no longer drops the whole command on an out-of-set value. Otherwise, document in the IPC contract that adding a value to any of these four strict enums (and to TagMode) is a breaking change requiring app+engine to ship in lockstep. Given the two binaries ship as one signed bundle, the documentation route is a defensible no-code option.

confirmed.title: Missing REQUIRED payload fields are silently zero/null-filled by System.Text.Json instead of surfaced
confirmed.description: The C# payload records use positional constructor parameters with no `required` modifier. When a schema-required field is absent on the wire, System.Text.Json fills it with default(T) (0 / false / null) rather than throwing. I verified this: a `progress` event missing the required `processed` field decodes successfully with processed=0 (no exception). This is the OPPOSITE of the dimension's 'required field absent => exception that kills the pipe' worry — the pipe is robust, but a malformed/partial frame that lost a required scalar is masked as a legitimate zero value rather than skipped. For high-frequency progress/batchSummary events on the 4 GB box this could surface as a transient stall-to-zero in the UI counters rather than a dropped frame.
confirmed.impact: Low. No correctness loss under normal operation (the engine always emits required fields). The risk is diagnostic: a genuine engine bug that omits a required field, or a partially-corrupted frame, is silently absorbed as default-valued data (e.g. processed=0, failed=0, filesPerSecond=0) instead of being rejected, making such bugs invisible.
confirmed.fix: If strict frame validation is desired, mark schema-required payload fields with the C# `required` modifier (or [JsonRequired]) so STJ throws on absence (the throw is then caught+skipped by StdoutLoopAsync, same as any malformed line). Otherwise this is acceptable lenient behavior — document it.
confirmed.evidence: Dtos.cs:106-116 ScanProgress positional record, no `required` on Processed et al.; empirical decode test: `progress` payload with `processed` omitted -> OK (ProgressEvent, no throw).

verdict.reasoning:
I independently verified the finding by reading the three sources and running an empirical decode test against the real DTOs + converters.

SCHEMA (ipc.schema.json:354): ScanProgress's `_0` object marks `["sessionID","phase","total","discovered","processed","failed","filesPerSecond","residentMB","availableMB"]` as REQUIRED (only `etaSeconds` is optional/nullable).

C# (Dtos.cs:106-116): ScanProgress is a positional record with NO `required` modifier and NO `[JsonRequired]` on any parameter. The decode path is IpcCoder.Decode<IpcEvent> -> EventPayloadJsonConverter -> ReadWrapped<ScanProgress> -> JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ScanProgress> (EventPayload.cs:78, 169). IpcCoder.Options sets PropertyNameCaseInsensitive=false but nothing that enforces required-on-absence.

EMPIRICAL TEST (probe referencing the real FileID.IpcSchema assembly, net8.0, then deleted): a `progress` frame missing `processed` decoded OK with processed=0 (no throw). Missing processed+failed+filesPerSecond -> processed=0 failed=0 fps=0. Missing required string sessionID -> session='' (empty). This reproduces the finding's claim exactly: System.Text.Json supplies default(T) for an absent schema-required positional parameter rather than throwing.

DECODE LOOP (EngineClient.cs:670-678): a parse exception IS caught and the line skipped, so the finding's premise that a JsonException would be safely caught+skipped is also correct.

So this is NOT casing/optionality DRIFT between the three sources (the field names and types all match). It is a real contract-ENFORCEMENT gap: the schema says these scalars are required, but the C# decoder treats them as optional-with-default. The finding self-categorizes it correctly as ipc-decode-robustness, not drift.

Severity is genuinely low, and the finding is honest about that. Under normal operation there is zero correctness loss: the engine serializes ScanProgress as a Rust struct via serde, which emits ALL fields unconditionally, so a required scalar is never omitted in practice. The risk is purely diagnostic — a hypothetical engine bug that drops a required field, or a torn frame that happens to remain valid JSON minus exactly one scalar key, would be masked as a legitimate 0 rather than rejected. A torn frame is far more likely to fail JSON tokenization outright (caught at line 674) than to produce a valid object missing one key, which further bounds the real-world impact. This is not a perf cliff — the cost is one extra default-fill, off any hot-path concern.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct and appropriately scoped. Marking the schema-required ScanProgress fields (and the analogous required fields on other high-frequency payloads like BatchSummary/ScanComplete) with the C# `required` modifier or `[JsonRequired]` makes System.Text.Json throw JsonException on absence, which is then caught and the frame skipped by the stdout loop (EngineClient.cs:674-677) — same path as any malformed line. The finding correctly limits this to schema-required fields, so it would NOT touch the intentionally-optional defaulted fields (HardwareInfo's V15.9 diagnostics, FileDoneEvent.SkippedStages, RestructurePlan.FolderClassifications, ScanProgress.EtaSeconds), which must stay defaulted for old-engine forward-compat. The finding also correctly presents this as optional hardening ('otherwise document it as acceptable lenient behavior'), which is the right call given the engine never actually omits these fields. One caveat the fix should note: `[JsonRequired]`/`required` only checks presence, not non-null — but that is sufficient here.
verdict.corrected_fix: No correction needed to the fix's mechanism. If applying it, scope it tightly to fields that appear in the schema's `required` arrays (e.g. on ScanProgress: SessionId, Phase, Total, Discovered, Processed, Failed, FilesPerSecond, ResidentMb, AvailableMb — but NOT EtaSeconds, which is `["number","null"]` in schema and `double?` in C#). Do the same for BatchSummary and ScanComplete if strict validation is wanted on those high-frequency events. Leave the deliberately-defaulted forward-compat fields (HardwareInfo extras, FileDoneEvent.SkippedStages, RestructurePlan.FolderClassifications) as optional. Given the engine emits all required fields unconditionally via serde, the equally-acceptable resolution is to simply document the lenient behavior and close as won't-fix.

confirmed.title: Schema documents alphabetical key ordering for byte-determinism, but the engine emits insertion-order keys
confirmed.description: The canonical schema's top-level description (ipc.schema.json:5) states 'JSON object keys are emitted in alphabetical order for byte-deterministic round-trips.' The Windows engine does NOT do this: write_frame (sink.rs:81-88) calls serde_json::to_writer, and the in-code comment (sink.rs:82-84) explicitly acknowledges 'Insertion-order keys are fine — consumers don't care about JSON key order. Byte-for-byte parity with sorted output would require a custom sorted-key writer.' The C# side likewise emits declaration-order keys. Both decoders (serde, System.Text.Json) are key-order independent, so this is harmless at runtime.
confirmed.impact: No functional impact on the Windows IPC path (no consumer relies on key order). It is a real contract/doc drift: the schema makes a byte-determinism guarantee the Windows implementations do not honor, which would bite anyone who builds a cross-platform golden-frame byte-comparison test or assumes canonical-form hashing of frames.
confirmed.fix: Either drop/qualify the 'alphabetical order ... byte-deterministic' clause in ipc.schema.json:5 to match reality, or note it applies only to the Swift/macOS reference encoder. No code change needed on Windows unless byte-determinism is actually a requirement.
confirmed.evidence: ipc.schema.json:5 description string; sink.rs:82-84 comment 'Insertion-order keys are fine ... Byte-for-byte parity with sorted output would require a custom sorted-key writer.' and sink.rs:85 serde_json::to_writer.

verdict.reasoning: Verified all three cited sources directly. (1) Schema: ipc.schema.json:5 contains verbatim "JSON object keys are emitted in alphabetical order for byte-deterministic round-trips." The same sentence is also duplicated in shared/ipc-schema/README.md:11. (2) Rust engine: sink.rs:81-88 write_frame calls serde_json::to_writer(&mut *w, event) on IpcEvent, which is a #[derive(Serialize)] struct (mod.rs:26-31). Serde serializes derived structs in field-DECLARATION order, never alphabetical. Concrete proof the engine output is non-alphabetical: IpcEvent declares `t` (line 29) before `payload` (line 30), so the wire emits {"t":...,"payload":...}; alphabetical ("payload" < "t") would emit payload first. The in-code comment at sink.rs:82-84 explicitly acknowledges this ("Insertion-order keys are fine... Byte-for-byte parity with sorted output would require a custom sorted-key writer."). I checked Cargo.toml:36 — serde_json is built with feature "preserve_order"; that feature only swaps the backing map for serde_json::Value/Map from BTreeMap to IndexMap and has NO effect on struct-field serialization order, so it does not sort either. No custom sorted-key serializer exists anywhere in src/engine/src/ipc. (3) C# mirror: IpcCoder.EncodeLine uses JsonSerializer.Serialize via Utf8JsonWriter with no sorting option (System.Text.Json has no built-in key sort and emits declaration/metadata order). So neither Windows implementation honors the schema's alphabetical/byte-deterministic guarantee. Runtime impact is nil because both decoders (serde, System.Text.Json) are key-order-independent and no consumer relies on key order — confirming the finding's own "harmless at runtime" claim. This is genuine doc/contract drift, not a functional bug.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct in direction and correctly identifies that no Windows code change is needed (the schema doc is wrong about reality, not the engine). It is the right place to fix — the schema/doc, not the encoder — since the byte-determinism guarantee is not actually a Windows requirement and adding a custom sorted-key writer would cost a per-frame allocation/sort on the hot stdout path for zero benefit. One gap: the finding cites only ipc.schema.json:5, but the identical false guarantee is also duplicated at shared/ipc-schema/README.md:11; a complete fix must amend both so the drift is fully resolved.
verdict.corrected_fix: In ipc.schema.json:5, change the clause "JSON object keys are emitted in alphabetical order for byte-deterministic round-trips." to reflect reality — e.g. "JSON object keys are emitted in struct/declaration order; both decoders are key-order independent, so frames are NOT guaranteed byte-identical across platform encoders (do not rely on canonical-form/byte-hash equality of frames)." Apply the same correction to the duplicated sentence in shared/ipc-schema/README.md:11. No engine or C# code change is warranted: byte-determinism is not a Windows IPC requirement, and a sorted-key writer would add an avoidable per-frame sort/alloc on the stdout hot path. If true cross-platform byte-determinism ever becomes a requirement, it must be implemented consistently in all three encoders (Swift/Rust/C#), not just documented.

confirmed.title: MobileCLIP embed/embed_batch fill the CHW tensor with scalar 4-D ndarray indexing per pixel
confirmed.description: Both embed() (mobileclip.rs:89-100) and embed_batch() (mobileclip.rs:148-161) build the (N,3,n,n) tensor with a per-pixel `for y/for x` loop computing a manual interleaved source index `i = (y*n + x)*3` and writing three `chw[[b,c,y,x]]` 4-D indexed scalars. input_size is 224 (set at mobileclip.rs:63 — note the const comment says 256 but the field is 224), so n=224 → 224*224 = 50,176 iterations * 3 = ~150K bounds-checked 4-D ndarray writes per image, *batch (default CLIP batch size 8). The source buffer is already a flat interleaved RGB slice and the destination ndarray is contiguous, so the channel planes can be written linearly. CLIP runs on every image (semantic-search embedding) so this is squarely on the per-file hot path.
confirmed.impact: CLIP is the downstream stage that the file's own STATS instrumentation notes is already starved behind RAM++ on the 4 GB card (batch_clip.rs:42-49). Reducing CLIP CPU preprocess won't move the RAM++-bound throughput ceiling, but it lowers per-file CPU and frees cores for the decode pool / concurrent RAM++ preprocess. The 4-D `chw[[b,c,y,x]]` indexing recomputes a 4-axis stride dot-product + 4 bounds checks per scalar where a linear plane write needs none.
confirmed.fix: Write into `chw.as_slice_mut()` (contiguous, C-order = [b][c][y][x]) with running offsets: for each image b, base = b*3*n*n; channel r plane at base, g at base+n*n, b at base+2*n*n; step the source index by 3. Identical numerical result, no per-scalar 4-D indexing.
confirmed.evidence: mobileclip.rs:90-100 (embed) and 149-161 (embed_batch) show the per-pixel index math `(y*n+x)*3` feeding three chw[[..]] writes; input_size=224 at line 63; default CLIP batch size 8 at batch_clip.rs:33.

verdict.reasoning: Every literal claim in the finding checks out against the actual source. mobileclip.rs:89-100 (embed) and 148-161 (embed_batch) both build the (N,3,n,n) tensor with a nested `for y in 0..n / for x in 0..n` loop, compute the interleaved source index `let i = (y*n+x)*3`, and write three 4-D-indexed scalars `chw[[b,c,y,x]] = (v - CLIP_MEAN[c]) / CLIP_STD[c]`. input_size is 224 (mobileclip.rs:63) — so n=224, 50,176 pixels x 3 = 150,528 bounds-checked 4-D ndarray writes per image. The const/comment discrepancy is real: lines 1-2, 61-62, 74, 121-128 say "256x256" but the field is 224. This IS on the per-file scan hot path: tagging.rs:1461 (pool path, `c.embed`) and tagging.rs:1484 (batch path, `clip_coord.embed(...).await` -> ClipBatchCoordinator -> embed_batch). Input is a flat interleaved RGB Vec<u8> from resize_rgb_quality (tagging.rs:1973, returns dst_w*dst_h*3), confirming the source-layout assumption. DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE=8 (batch_clip.rs:33). The proposed as_slice_mut() fix is numerically correct: Array4::<f32>::zeros((batch,3,n,n)) is standard C-contiguous layout with memory order [b][c][y][x], so a linear plane write with running offsets (base=b*3*n*n; r at base, g at base+n*n, b at base+2*n*n; index base+c*n*n+(y*n+x)) produces identical values with no per-scalar 4-axis stride dot-product or bounds check. So the drift/inefficiency is REAL and the fix is CORRECT. However the SEVERITY is overstated. The only cost removed is the ndarray 4-D addressing + bounds-check overhead per scalar (~1-3 ms/image at most); the float conversions and (r-mean)/std arithmetic — the bulk of the loop — are unchanged. The file's own STATS instrumentation (batch_clip.rs:42-49) PINS the throughput ceiling at RAM++ Swin-L (~670 ms/file on a 2-session VRAM-clamped pool); CLIP (~190 ms) sits downstream and starved (only ~2 files ever concurrently in the CLIP stage). The finding itself concedes "Reducing CLIP CPU preprocess won't move the RAM++-bound throughput ceiling." So this yields no measurable files/s gain — at best marginal CPU relief that already overlaps GPU inference. A correct, safe micro-optimization, not a perf cliff. Net: confirmed as a real (low-value) inefficiency, severity = low, not medium.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct and safe. Array4::zeros yields a standard-layout (row-major / C-contiguous) array, so as_slice_mut() returns Some with element order [b][c][y][x] (last axis fastest), and the offset scheme described (base=b*3*n*n; r-plane at base, g at base+n*n, b at base+2*n*n; per-pixel index = base + c*n*n + (y*n+x)) reproduces the exact same numeric writes with no per-scalar 4-D stride computation or bounds check. Numerically identical output, no clippy/borrow issues. The only nit: handle the as_slice_mut() Option (it is always Some for a freshly-zeroed standard-layout array, but unwrap/expect with an invariant note is cleaner than assuming). Worth doing as a cheap, low-risk cleanup, but it should be filed as severity=low (no throughput movement), not medium engine-hot-loop-perf.
verdict.corrected_fix: In both embed() and embed_batch(), replace the per-pixel 4-D `chw[[b,c,y,x]]` writes with linear plane writes into the contiguous backing slice. e.g. for embed_batch: `let plane = n * n; let buf = chw.as_slice_mut().expect("Array4::zeros is standard-layout/contiguous"); for (b, rgb) in rgb_256_images.iter().enumerate() { let base = b * 3 * plane; let mut i = 0usize; for p in 0..plane { let r = rgb[i] as f32 / 255.0; let g = rgb[i+1] as f32 / 255.0; let bch = rgb[i+2] as f32 / 255.0; buf[base + p] = (r - CLIP_MEAN[0]) / CLIP_STD[0]; buf[base + plane + p] = (g - CLIP_MEAN[1]) / CLIP_STD[1]; buf[base + 2*plane + p] = (bch - CLIP_MEAN[2]) / CLIP_STD[2]; i += 3; } }` (drop the y/x split entirely since the destination plane is itself row-major). Same change for embed() with base=0, single image. While here, fix the stale "256x256" comments (lines 1-2, 61-62, 74, 121-128) to 224x224 to match input_size=224. File under severity=low: correct micro-opt, no measurable files/s gain given the RAM++-bound ceiling.

confirmed.title: All three model preprocess paths zero-fill the input tensor (Array4::zeros) then overwrite every element
confirmed.description: ram_plus.rs:432 (`Array4::<f32>::zeros((1,3,n,n))`), mobileclip.rs:89/148, and sface.rs:78 all allocate the input tensor via `Array4::zeros`, which calloc-zeroes the buffer, and then the loop writes EVERY element. For RAM++ that is a 384*384*3*4 = ~1.7 MB zero-fill per image that is immediately fully overwritten; for CLIP ~600 KB/image (224²) *batch; per face ~150 KB. The zero-fill is wasted work on the per-file path. NOTE: in the *batched* RAM++ path (tag_batch, ram_plus.rs:345) the zeros() IS load-bearing because `slice_mut(...).assign(&chw)` only fills filled rows — leave that one; the single-image preprocess overwrites the whole array unconditionally so the zero-init is dead there.
confirmed.impact: One redundant ~1.7 MB memset per image (RAM++) on the per-file CPU path, plus the allocation. Small relative to the GPU forward but it is pure dead work and compounds with the indexing waste above on the same buffer. On the 4 GB / low-RAM target every avoided per-file allocation+memset reduces allocator pressure.
confirmed.fix: Use `Array4::uninit((1,3,n,n))` + assume_init after the fill loop writes all elements, or build the tensor from a pre-sized Vec<f32> filled in the loop and `Array4::from_shape_vec`. Only safe where the loop provably writes every element — the single-image RAM++/CLIP/SFace preprocess loops do; do NOT change the batched tag_batch path which relies on the zeros for unfilled slots.
confirmed.evidence: ram_plus.rs:432 Array4::zeros((1,3,n,n)) immediately followed by a full y/x overwrite loop (lines 433-440); same shape in mobileclip.rs:89,148 and sface.rs:78. Contrast tag_batch at ram_plus.rs:345 which legitimately needs zeros.

verdict.reasoning: I read all four cited sites and confirmed the factual claims. ram_plus.rs:432 is `Array4::<f32>::zeros((1,3,n,n))` (n=384) immediately followed by a loop (433-440) that writes channels 0,1,2 at every [y,x], i.e. all 3*n*n elements — the zero-init is provably dead. ~1.77 MB (384²·3·4). sface.rs:78 is the same shape for (1,3,112,112)=~150 KB and IS on the face path. The finding's instruction to leave ram_plus.rs:345 (tag_batch) is correct — that one uses slice_mut(...).assign(...). Two refinements that make the finding MORE right and partly LESS impactful: (1) line 432's single-image preprocess is reached on EVERY image even in the batched HW-4 path, because tag_batch (line 354) calls Self::preprocess per frame — so the dead memset is unconditionally per-file, as claimed. (2) mobileclip.rs:89 (single-image embed) is marked #[allow(dead_code)] (line 79) and its doc says the scan pipeline uses embed_batch, so that specific CLIP site the finding cites is OFF the hot path; the truly-hot CLIP equivalent is mobileclip.rs:148 (embed_batch), which the finding did not cite but has the identical dead zero-init. So the CLIP citation (line 89) is technically off-hot-path. The drift/waste itself is real at every cited line. On magnitude: a ~1.77 MB memset is single-digit microseconds; the per-image GPU forward is ~670 ms (per the code's own comment) and the bounds-checked 442k-element fill loop dwarfs the memset. So this is genuinely dead work but its throughput contribution is negligible — it will not move the 140 files/s target. The finding self-rates 'low' and frames it as 'small relative to the GPU forward but pure dead work,' which is an honest characterization. Confirmed as a real (low) finding, not refuted.
verdict.fix_assessment: The fix is directionally correct and the loops do provably write every element (single-image preprocess loops cover the full tensor), so uninit+assume_init or from_shape_vec is sound there. Two caveats: (1) `Array4::uninit(...)` + `.assume_init()` introduces `unsafe` into a per-file path to save single-digit microseconds against a 670 ms/img forward — a poor clarity/risk tradeoff in a codebase that defaults to no-unsafe; the `Array4::from_shape_vec` with a Vec filled in the loop is the safer, comment-free variant and is preferable. (2) The fix omits mobileclip.rs:148 (embed_batch), which is the actually-hot CLIP site and has the identical dead zero-init (every batch row is fully written since all buffers are validated full-size); mobileclip.rs:89 cited by the finding is #[allow(dead_code)] and off the hot path. The instruction to leave ram_plus.rs:345 (tag_batch) zeros() alone is correct.
verdict.corrected_fix: Prefer the safe form over unsafe uninit on this per-file path. For each single-image preprocess (ram_plus.rs:432, sface.rs:78) and the batched embed_batch (mobileclip.rs:148): build a Vec<f32> of length 3*n*n (or batch*3*n*n) and push/index-fill it in the existing loop order, then `Array4::from_shape_vec((1,3,n,n), v)?` — no zero-init, no unsafe. If using uninit for max speed, only do so where the loop provably writes every element (these single-image/full-batch loops qualify); keep `#[allow(dead_code)]` mobileclip.rs:89 and the load-bearing ram_plus.rs:345 tag_batch zeros() unchanged. Given the negligible throughput impact (memset is ~0.001% of the 670 ms/img forward), treat this as an optional cleanup, not a perf fix — do not add unsafe for it.

confirmed.title: Opt-in RAM++ batch path clones the full decoded frame per file before submitting to the coordinator
confirmed.description: When the opt-in RAM++ batch coordinator is active (`models.ram_plus_batch.is_some()`, enabled only by FILEID_RAMPLUS_BATCH_SIZE>1), the per-file body does `ram_coord.tag(rgb.clone(), w, h).await` — a full-frame clone of the decoded RGB8 buffer (e.g. an 8 MP image = ~24 MB; capped at MAX_DECODED_PIXELS = 50 MP = ~150 MB). The clone is needed only because `rgb` is still borrowed later (dHash already ran, but OCR at line 1674 also clones rgb, and the function holds `rgb` for the scene/enriched stages). The DEFAULT single-image pool path correctly avoids this — it calls `preprocess_tensor(&rgb, w, h)` borrowing the frame. So this waste is confined to the non-default batch path, which the in-tree measurements (ram_plus_batch.rs:9-16) already found REGRESSES on the 4 GB RTX 2060. Combined with the batch channel carrying full frames (REQUEST_CHANNEL_CAP=256), enabling the batch path both clones per file AND pins up to 256 full frames in the queue.
confirmed.impact: On the default config this is dormant (batch path off). If a user enables FILEID_RAMPLUS_BATCH_SIZE>1 on the 4 GB box (the exact knob the comments warn against), each file pays a 24-150 MB heap clone plus the deep full-frame queue — a real memory cliff on the low-RAM target. Severity kept low because it is off by default and already documented as not-recommended for this card.
confirmed.fix: If the batch path is ever made viable for a card, pass ownership of `rgb` into the coordinator by moving it (the only later reader on the same frame is OCR's rgb.clone() at line 1674 and the dHash which runs earlier — restructure so the batch submission is the last consumer and moves rgb), or have the coordinator preprocess to the (3,384,384) tensor on the worker before submit (like the pool path does) so only the ~1.7 MB tensor crosses the channel, not the full frame. Until then, leave the default off and keep the warning.
confirmed.evidence: tagging.rs:1520 `ram_coord.tag(rgb.clone(), w, h)`; contrast the default pool branch at tagging.rs:1527 `preprocess_tensor(&rgb, w, h)` (borrow). ram_plus_batch.rs:50-59 documents the full-frame REQUEST_CHANNEL_CAP=256 and Low-tier clamp; ram_plus_batch.rs:9-16 documents the measured regression on the 4 GB card.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified every checkable claim against the actual sources.

1. The clone is real and a full frame. tagging.rs:1324 destructures `if let Some((rgb, w, h)) = image_source` so `rgb` is an OWNED `Vec<u8>` (produced by `rgb.into_raw()` at tagging.rs:1140 / `frame.rgb` at 1160, the full decoded RGB8 with no upstream downscale). The batch branch at tagging.rs:1520 calls `Some(ram_coord.tag(rgb.clone(), w, h).await)` — `RamPlusBatchCoordinator::tag(&self, rgb: Vec<u8>, ...)` (ram_plus_batch.rs:116) takes the buffer BY VALUE, so `rgb.clone()` is a genuine full-frame heap copy.

2. The default pool path correctly avoids it. tagging.rs:1521-1528 (the `else if let Some(ram_pool)` branch) calls `RamPlusTagger::preprocess_tensor(&rgb, w, h)` — a BORROW. Confirmed contrast.

3. The clone is forced by a later reader. A grep of `\brgb\b` shows `rgb` is read again at tagging.rs:1674 `run_ocr_blocking(rgb.clone(), w, h)`, a statement that executes AFTER line 1520 on the same frame. All other readers (dHash 1345, faces 1365/1392/1393, CLIP 1458/1483) run BEFORE 1520. So a naive move at 1520 would not compile — the clone is structurally required given current ordering, exactly as the finding states.

4. Off by default. `configured_batch_size()` (ram_plus_batch.rs:83-84) reads `FILEID_RAMPLUS_BATCH_SIZE` defaulting to 0; the coordinator is only built when `ram_batch_size > 1` (tagging.rs:535), in which case `ram_plus_batch = Some` and `ram_plus = None` (mutually exclusive, tagging.rs:534-569). With the env unset, `models.ram_plus_batch` is None and line 1520 is dead. Dormant on default config — confirmed.

5. Deep full-frame queue. ram_plus_batch.rs:51-55 documents REQUEST_CHANNEL_CAP=256 carrying FULL frames (RamPlusRequest{ rgb: Vec<u8> } at lines 67-72), clamped to REQUEST_CHANNEL_CAP_LOW=16 under MemoryTier::Low (lines 59, 101-104). So enabling the path both clones per file AND can pin up to 256 (or 16 on Low) full frames. Confirmed.

6. Measured regression. ram_plus_batch.rs:9-16 documents batched=4 at 1.6 files/s vs single-pool 2.1 f/s (~23% slower) on the 4 GB RTX 2060, GPU already saturated at batch=1. Confirmed in-tree.

7. Size math. MAX_DECODED_PIXELS = 50_000_000 (tagging.rs:1853) → ~150 MB RGB8 ceiling; the code itself uses TYPICAL_FRAME_MB = 24 (~8 MP RGB8) at tagging.rs:702 — the finding's 24-150 MB range matches the codebase's own figures exactly.

The only defect in the finding is a citation typo: it lists the file as `pipeline/ram_plus_batch.rs`, but it is actually `models/ram_plus_batch.rs` (the cited line numbers 9-16 and 50-59 are correct within that file). Cosmetic; does not affect substance.

This is a genuine memory-allocation waste confined to the opt-in batch path, on the per-file hot loop, on the low-RAM target — but it is dormant by default and already documented as not-recommended for this card, so it is not a live cliff in shipped configuration. No IPC-contract drift is involved; this is purely an engine perf finding and is correctly categorized as such.
verdict.fix_assessment: The fix is correct in direction but understates the work for one of its two options. Option A (move `rgb` into the coordinator instead of cloning): valid, but requires reordering so the batch submit is the LAST reader of `rgb` — currently OCR at tagging.rs:1674 reads `rgb` after line 1520, so simply changing `rgb.clone()` to `rgb` would fail to compile. You'd have to run OCR before the batch submit (or buffer/restructure), which interleaves with the GPU-bound await and is a non-trivial reordering. Option B (preprocess to the (3,384,384) ~1.7 MB tensor on the worker before submit, like the pool path): also valid and arguably cleaner, but it is NOT just a call-site change — the coordinator currently borrows raw frames in run_coordinator (ram_plus_batch.rs:174 `r.rgb.as_slice()`) and preprocessing happens inside `tagger.tag_batch`; moving preprocess to the worker means changing RamPlusRequest's payload from Vec<u8> to the CHW tensor, changing `tag()`'s signature, and adding a `tag_batch_prepared`-style entry that skips the resize+normalize. Both are real restructures, not one-liners. The finding's own conclusion — leave the default off and keep the warning until a card is validated — is the right call and matches the existing in-tree guidance. Severity rating of "low" is honest given the path is off by default and documented as a regression on the reference 4 GB box.
verdict.corrected_fix: Keep the path off by default (no change needed for shipped config). If/when a high-SM/high-VRAM card is validated and the batch path is enabled, prefer Option B: make the tagging worker run `RamPlusTagger::preprocess_tensor(&rgb, w, h)` (borrow, exactly as the pool path does at tagging.rs:1527) and submit only the resulting ~1.7 MB (3,384,384) CHW tensor across the channel. This (a) eliminates the 24-150 MB per-file clone at tagging.rs:1520 since preprocess borrows `rgb`, and (b) shrinks the 256-deep REQUEST_CHANNEL_CAP queue from up to ~6 GB of full frames to ~435 MB of tensors — directly addressing both the clone and the deep-queue memory pins. This requires: change RamPlusRequest.rgb: Vec<u8> to a prepared tensor field, change RamPlusBatchCoordinator::tag's signature accordingly, and have run_coordinator feed the prepared tensors to a tag_batch_prepared path (skipping the in-coordinator resize/normalize). Option A (move rgb instead of clone by reordering OCR before the batch submit) also works but is more fragile because it couples stage ordering to the borrow checker. Also correct the finding's file path: it is platforms/windows/src/engine/src/models/ram_plus_batch.rs, not pipeline/ram_plus_batch.rs.

confirmed.title: dbwriter clones the per-face embedding bytes to satisfy the legacy print_data bind
confirmed.description: In the per-face insert loop, `arcface_bytes` (the 128-d SFace embedding → 512 bytes, from floats_to_le_bytes) is bound twice — once as `print_data` and once as `arcface_embedding` — using `arcface_bytes.clone()` for the first bind (line 379) and the owned value for the second (line 381). The clone duplicates 512 bytes per face per batch. rusqlite binds blobs by reference (params! borrows), so both binds could reference the same `&arcface_bytes` slice without cloning: bind `arcface_bytes.as_slice()` for both positions.
confirmed.impact: 512-byte heap clone per detected face per batch flush. Tiny in absolute terms (faces are a fraction of files and few per image) but it is a needless allocation inside the single-writer DB transaction — strictly avoidable.
confirmed.fix: Bind both params from the same slice: `params![file_id, arcface_bytes.as_slice(), bbox_json, arcface_bytes.as_slice(), face.quality as f64]` (or `&arcface_bytes`), dropping the `.clone()`. The bytes are identical by the file's own comment ('print_data legacy: same bytes as arcface_embedding').
confirmed.evidence: dbwriter.rs:374 `let arcface_bytes = floats_to_le_bytes(&face.embedding);` then line 379 `arcface_bytes.clone()` and line 381 `arcface_bytes` in the same params! macro; comment at line 375 confirms the two columns hold identical bytes; sface.rs:107 confirms 128-d → 512 bytes.

verdict.reasoning:
I read all three load-bearing sources and confirmed the drift/alloc is real.

1) dbwriter.rs:374-384 (verified verbatim): `let arcface_bytes = floats_to_le_bytes(&face.embedding);` (line 374), comment "print_data legacy: same bytes as arcface_embedding" (375), then `face_stmt.execute(params![ file_id, arcface_bytes.clone() /*line 379*/, bbox_json, arcface_bytes /*line 381*/, face.quality as f64 ])`. The two blob columns (print_data=?2, arcface_embedding=?4 per INSERT_FACE_SQL at dbwriter.rs:693-696) are bound to identical bytes, one via `.clone()`.

2) floats_to_le_bytes (dbwriter.rs:702-708) returns `Vec<u8>` of `v.len()*4` bytes.

3) SFace dimension: models/sface.rs (not the path the finding cited — it said sface.rs:107 but the file is engine/src/models/sface.rs; the line number 107 is still correct) asserts `emb.len() != 128` at line 107 with comment "128-d / 512-byte blobs". So the embedding is 128 f32 -> 512 bytes. The `.clone()` therefore duplicates exactly 512 bytes per face.

4) The load-bearing claim — that rusqlite binds by reference so the clone is avoidable — is PROVEN by the macro definition (rusqlite 0.31, Cargo.toml:43): `macro_rules! params { ($($param:expr),+ $(,)?) => { &[$(&$param as &dyn ToSql),+] ... } }`. Each argument is taken as `&$param` (a borrow). So `arcface_bytes.as_slice()` (or `&arcface_bytes`) can be used in BOTH positions — two immutable borrows of the same Vec, fully legal, no clone, no move. The proposed fix compiles.

Conclusion: the needless 512-byte heap allocation per detected face per batch flush is real, on the single-writer DB transaction path, and strictly avoidable. The finding is accurate, including its file/line citations (dbwriter.rs:379 clone confirmed) and its own self-assessment that the absolute cost is tiny.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct and compiles. `params![file_id, arcface_bytes.as_slice(), bbox_json, arcface_bytes.as_slice(), face.quality as f64]` works because `&[u8]: ToSql` and `params!` borrows each arg (`&$param as &dyn ToSql`), so two immutable borrows of the same `Vec` are legal. Equivalent simpler form `&arcface_bytes` twice (Vec<u8>: ToSql) also works. Drops the 512-byte clone with no behavioral change — both columns still receive identical bytes, consistent with the print_data-legacy comment.
verdict.corrected_fix:
Replace dbwriter.rs:377-383:
    .execute(params![
        file_id,
        arcface_bytes.as_slice(),
        bbox_json,
        arcface_bytes.as_slice(),
        face.quality as f64,
    ])
This binds both print_data (?2) and arcface_embedding (?4) from the same slice, eliminating the `.clone()` at line 379. No other change needed; the bytes are identical by design.

confirmed.title: Model pool is sized from the unclamped CPU worker cap, not the Low-tier-clamped pipeline worker count (benign, but the decoupling is implicit)
confirmed.description: `models_worker_count = platform::default_worker_cap()` (unclamped, e.g. 12 on an 8-core box) is passed to `ModelStack::load_default`, while the actual pipeline `worker_count` is separately clamped to `min(6)` under MemoryTier::Low at line 272. `resolve_pool_size(models_worker_count)` ends with `.min(worker_count.max(1))`, so on a Low-RAM box the unclamped value (12) becomes the pool ceiling instead of the clamped 6. This is NOT a live bug because the Low-tier branch inside resolve_pool_size forces `cap = cap.min(1)` first, and the VRAM gate clamps to ~2 on a 6 GB card — so the effective pool is 1-2 regardless of whether 12 or 6 is passed. Pool arrays are indexed `worker_idx % pool.len()`, so the count mismatch is harmless by design (documented at scan.rs:269-271). Flagging only because the two worker-count values silently diverge and a future change to resolve_pool_size's clamp order could turn this latent.
confirmed.impact: No current impact (Low-tier + VRAM clamps dominate). Latent risk: if the Low-tier `cap.min(1)` or VRAM clamp were ever relaxed, passing the unclamped 12 would let the pool exceed what the Low-tier box's RAM budget intends.
confirmed.fix: Pass the same memory-tier-clamped worker count to load_default that the pipeline uses, computing the clamped `worker_count` before the model load at line 150 and reusing it, so pool sizing and pipeline concurrency derive from one source of truth.

verdict.reasoning:
Every factual claim in the finding is accurate, verified against the actual source:

1. scan.rs:150 — `let models_worker_count = platform::default_worker_cap() as usize;` is the UNCLAMPED CPU worker cap. Confirmed.
2. scan.rs:163 — that unclamped value is passed to `ModelStack::load_default(models_worker_count)`. Confirmed.
3. scan.rs:272-275 — the actual pipeline `worker_count` is SEPARATELY computed and clamped: `MemoryTier::Low => default_worker_cap().min(6)`, non-Low uses the full cap. So the two worker-count values genuinely diverge on a Low-tier box (e.g. 6 vs 12). Confirmed.
4. tagging.rs:426-427 — `load_default(worker_count)` -> `resolve_pool_size(worker_count)`, so the unclamped value flows into pool sizing. Confirmed.
5. resolve_pool_size (tagging.rs:355-392): clamp order is (a) cap = MODEL_POOL_SIZE(4)/env; (b) VRAM gate — on a 6 GB card `(6144-1500)/2000 = 2`, VRAM-unreadable -> `cap.min(1)=1`; (c) line 388-390 Low-tier `cap = cap.min(1)`; (d) line 391 `cap.max(1).min(worker_count.max(1))`. The `worker_count` argument ONLY influences the result at line 391. Confirmed exactly as described.

The finding's own thesis holds: on the reference 4 GB / MemoryTier::Low box, `cap` is forced to 1 by the Low-tier clamp at line 389 BEFORE line 391, so `1.min(12)` and `1.min(6)` are identical — the unclamped 12 never escalates the pool. The VRAM gate independently dominates too (clamps to ~2 or 1). On NON-Low tiers, both the pipeline and the pool use the same unclamped `default_worker_cap()`, so there is no divergence at all there. Net: there is NO tier on which passing 12 vs 6 yields a different allocation. Pool access is always `worker_idx % pool.len()` (tagging.rs:1360-1361, 1457, 1538), so worker count and pool size are correctly decoupled by design.

I confirm the code structure exactly as the finding describes (verdict=confirmed for the drift's existence), but it is a latent, fully-dominated decoupling with zero current performance or correctness impact — exactly as the finding self-states ("NOT a live bug", confidence 0.4, severity low).
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct and harmless but unnecessary for any current behavior. It suggests computing the memory-tier-clamped `worker_count` before line 150 and reusing it for both `load_default` and `ScanSession`, giving a single source of truth. This is a valid behavior-preserving refactor: on Low tier the result of `resolve_pool_size` is dominated by the `cap.min(1)` at tagging.rs:389 and the VRAM gate, so feeding it 6 instead of 12 still yields a pool of 1; on non-Low tiers both values are already the same unclamped cap. So the fix changes nothing observable — it only removes the implicit decoupling and the latent risk that a future relaxation of the line-389 Low-tier clamp or the VRAM gate could let the unclamped 12 raise the pool ceiling. It is a defensible defensive cleanup, not a perf fix.
verdict.corrected_fix: No correction needed to the fix's mechanics — it is sound. The only correction is to expectations: it yields ZERO measurable change on the 4 GB reference box (or any current tier), because `resolve_pool_size` already clamps the pool to 1-2 via the Low-tier and VRAM gates regardless of whether 12 or 6 is passed. Treat it as a maintainability/single-source-of-truth cleanup guarding a latent risk, not a performance correction. If applied, hoist the line-272-275 `worker_count` computation above line 150 and pass it to `load_default`.

uncertain.title: RAM++ preprocess uses get_pixel + scalar 4-D ndarray indexing on the primary per-file tagger path (~442K bounds-checked ops/image)
uncertain.description: preprocess() — called for EVERY scanned image on the default RAM++ tagger path (both the single-image pool via preprocess_tensor() and tag_batch()) — fills the (1,3,384,384) input tensor with a double `for y/for x` loop that does `resized.get_pixel(x as u32, y as u32)` and three scalar `chw[[0,c,y,x]]` assignments per pixel. INPUT_SIZE=384, so this is 384*384 = 147,456 iterations, each performing one `get_pixel` (an ImageBuffer index computation `(y*width + x)*channels` with a bounds check + slice reslice) and three `ndarray` 4-D indexed writes. Each `chw[[0,c,y,x]]` recomputes the full 4-D stride dot-product (`s0*0 + s1*c + s2*y + s3*x`) and bounds-checks 4 axes — ~442K such indexed writes + 147K get_pixel calls per image. `image::imageops::resize` already returns an ImageBuffer whose backing store is a contiguous interleaved RGB `Vec<u8>` reachable via `.as_raw()`/`.into_raw()` (the file already uses `from_raw` at line 423), so the pixels could be walked as a flat `&[u8]` and written into three contiguous channel planes of `chw` via `ndarray`'s `as_slice_mut()` with a single linear index — eliminating the per-pixel get_pixel reslice and collapsing the 4-D stride math to a running counter.
uncertain.impact: This is pure CPU work on the primary tagger's per-file path. While RAM++'s GPU forward (~670 ms) dominates wall time on the 4 GB DirectML box, the preprocess is deliberately run OUTSIDE the GPU permit/session lock (see tagging.rs:1527 comment) precisely so it can overlap other workers' GPU forwards on the [2,12] decoder/worker CPU pool. On a low-core 4 GB box the per-pixel get_pixel + scalar 4-D indexing inflates that overlap window and steals CPU from concurrent decode, narrowing the overlap the design relies on. Cutting ~590K indexed/bounds-checked ops per image to a flat slice walk is a meaningful constant-factor win with byte-identical output.
uncertain.fix: Pull the resized pixels as a contiguous slice (`let buf = resized.as_raw();` or `into_raw()`), then fill chw via its contiguous backing store: get `chw.as_slice_mut()` and write the three CHW planes with a single running source index `i` stepping by 3, e.g. plane r at offset 0, g at n*n, b at 2*n*n; OR iterate `Zip` over `chw.outer_iter_mut`/`azip!`. This removes the get_pixel reslice and replaces every `chw[[..]]` 4-D index with a linear write. Output stays byte-identical (same Triangle resize, same ImageNet normalize).
uncertain.evidence: ram_plus.rs:433-440 double loop with resized.get_pixel(x,y) and chw[[0,c,y,x]] writes; line 423 already constructs ImageBuffer via from_raw, confirming the resized buffer is a contiguous Vec<u8>. preprocess is invoked by preprocess_tensor (tagging.rs:1527, default pool path) and tag_batch (ram_plus.rs:354) for every image.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding's CODE description is fully confirmed by reading the actual file. ram_plus.rs:432-440 fills Array4::<f32>::zeros((1,3,384,384)) with a double for y/for x loop calling resized.get_pixel(x,y) and three scalar chw[[0,c,y,x]] 4-D indexed writes — ~147,456 iterations × (1 get_pixel + 3 indexed writes) ≈ 590K bounds-checked ops/image. INPUT_SIZE=384 (line 31). Both cited call paths are real and on the per-file tagger path: preprocess_tensor (single-image pool, tagging.rs:1527) and tag_batch (batch coordinator, ram_plus.rs:354) both call preprocess. The overlap design is real: preprocess runs outside the GPU permit/session lock (tagging.rs:1522-1527 comment). The proposed fix is technically CORRECT and byte-identical: image 0.25's imageops::resize returns ImageBuffer<Rgb<u8>,Vec<u8>> with contiguous interleaved backing via .as_raw() (the file already uses from_raw at 423), and Array4::zeros is C-contiguous so as_slice_mut()==Some with CHW plane offsets 0/n²/2n². So this is a legitimate, safe micro-optimization.

WHERE THE FINDING OVERREACHES is the severity and impact. It is rated 'high' and claims the per-pixel cost 'narrows the overlap the design relies on' and 'steals CPU from concurrent decode'. The actual architecture refutes the impact magnitude: (1) the RAM++ GPU forward dominates at ~670 ms/image (ram_plus.rs:333) → ~6-8 files/s, GPU-bound; (2) decode runs on a SEPARATE Stage-1b decoder pool, not the worker pool that does preprocess; (3) the code's own comment at tagging.rs:695-696 explicitly states decode '(CPU, the [2,12] decoder pool) vastly outruns the GPU-bound RAM++ tagger... so the channel sits full all scan'; (4) preprocess runs on LOWEST-priority tokio workers (tagging.rs:776) that spend ~99% of per-file time blocked on the GPU permit/session Mutex. The 147K-pixel loop costs on the order of tens-to-low-hundreds of µs against a 670,000 µs GPU forward — roughly 0.01–0.05% of per-file RAM++ wall time, overlapping CPU that is otherwise idle waiting on the GPU. Scan throughput and the ≥140 files/s goal are gated by the GPU forward, not this loop. So the drift/perf-claim is REAL as code but the cliff is negligible; it is not a 'high' and not a meaningful constant-factor throughput win. Verdict uncertain rather than confirmed because the optimization is genuinely valid and worth doing as hygiene, but refuted as a high-severity perf cliff.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct and safe. image 0.25's imageops::resize returns an ImageBuffer backed by a contiguous interleaved Vec<u8> (as_raw()/into_raw()); Array4::<f32>::zeros is standard (C) layout so as_slice_mut() returns Some, and the flat CHW index ((0*3+c)*384+y)*384+x gives plane offsets r=0, g=n², b=2n². Walking the source slice with a running index stepping by 3 and writing the three planes is byte-identical (same Triangle resize, same ImageNet mean/std normalize). It removes the per-pixel get_pixel reslice and the 4-D stride/bounds math. The only correction is to the framing: this is a hygiene micro-opt, not a throughput-moving fix — the per-file RAM++ path is GPU-forward-bound (~670 ms) and the preprocess overlaps otherwise-idle, GPU-permit-blocked worker CPU, so expected wall-time/throughput delta is effectively zero on the 4 GB DirectML reference box.
verdict.corrected_fix: Apply the finding's fix as written (it is valid and byte-identical): pull `let buf = resized.as_raw();` (or into_raw()), get `let out = chw.as_slice_mut().expect("Array4::zeros is contiguous");`, then loop a single source index `i` over the n*n pixels, writing out[i] (R plane), out[n*n + i] (G), out[2*n*n + i] (B) with the existing (v/255 - mean)/std normalize, advancing buf by 3 per pixel. Equivalently use ndarray Zip/azip! over the three plane slices. Keep this as a low-priority cleanup, NOT a high-severity perf fix — do not expect a measurable files/s change; the per-file path is bound by the ~670 ms RAM++ GPU forward and the loop overlaps GPU-permit-blocked worker CPU.
